I am sorry for the late response. I got some time today to revisit why I added file_content. 

Originally, file_content was intended to be place holder for the content in memory. Because my content came in as <str>, and I have not wrapped it with StringIO, hence opendoc confuses it with filename and  initializes fm as FileManager instead of BytesStreamManager. For that reason, I added 'file_content'. 

Your subsequent commit to remove 'file_content' is nicer solution. I am looking forward to the pypi release.

I found the problem:


     def unpack(self, buf):
        f = cStringIO.StringIO(buf)
        line = f.readline()
        l = line.strip().split()
        if len(l) != 3 or l[0] not in self.__methods or            not l[2].startswith(self.__proto):
            raise dpkt.UnpackError('invalid request: %r' % line)

However, in an HTTP/0.9 request, there is no protocol field.  An HTTP/0.9 GET request looks like

GET /

so the unpack method throws dpkt.UnpackErrors because there are only 2 elements in list l .  This is a problem for F5 HTTP monitors, which use HTTP/0.9 by default.

I like this proposal. Minor comments:
- Jinja2 is great. As a starting point, I've been using it here: https://github.com/imphil/fusesoc/tree/spyglass
- The common file extension for Jinja2 templates is ".j2", giving you editor highlighting in many editors.
- You need a way to specify the file type of the resulting HDL file. We have now some tools which need different code to add a SystemVerilog file vs. a Verilog file, or even special commands to add a SV-2009 vs. a SV-2001 file. I see two options:
  - Use the same file_type as today, "systemVerilog-2009" and invoke the templating logic whenever you detect a ".j2" file extension.
  - Use a combined file_type, e.g. "jinja2+systemVerilog-2009"
- I'd prefer YAML over JSON.
  - It allows adding comments to files.
  - YAML is planned to be used for CAPI2 description files, keeping the style across fusesoc the same.
- It'd be nice to be able to specify templating variables inside the .core file, as well as in a separate file. Or even merge the functionality with the params functionality that's currently present? In the end, it serves a very similar purpose.
- It also would be nice to introduce a bit of name-spacing from the start for the variables. This allows us to make pre-defined template variables available (e.g. build tool) as well as passing user-defined template variables on to the template.

@pavoni If the device is under subscription, it should not be necessary to call get_value() at all, because the value is being passed to the subscriber's callback when the device is updated. But the subscription loop does not update the cached copy of the sdata that the VeraDevice object is holding--it doesn't share its newly-gotten update with the cache--so the two will be out of sync at some point, and a client that separately calls get_value() is likely to get a different answer than a subscription callback for the same device passed shortly before. So, in effect, get_value() also requires polling (via Refresh=True) to keep consistent data with a subscription Neither get_value() nor get_complex_value() can be trusted, as Refresh=True must be used to bring them in sync. And so I view them as equals currently, except that the refresh function for get_value() takes a lot more processing (fetches all device data, and then has to search through it) than get_complex_value() (queries for a single device, shorter response and no search required).

But as I said, my intention is to fix the caching/change fetch so that it stays in sync with what a subscriber would see, and hopefully mostly obviates the need to call either get_value() or get_complex_value() (or its eventual successors if renamed) with Refresh=True. It is also the case that both sets of names (the real state names and the short codes) can be kept in sync in this way. It will take a little more work, but it is readily possible with available responses from Vera.

```
Generic[T, U]         Generic[T]
       ↑                  ↑
*FullUnparam*    ←   FullUnparam[T, int]  ←  FullUnparam[str, int]
       ↑                  ↑                       ↑            ↖ 
*FullUnparam2*       *HalfParam*         ←    HalfParam[str]       *EntirelyParam*
                                                  ↑
                                            *EntirelyParam2*
```

> In a well-formed annotation, the start time should be 0, which I've argued should never be counted as positive hits.

I wouldn't agree with that. As Thomas said, there may be two different situations. A file could start with silence:
0  1.2  Silence
1.2  10  A
or a file could start right away with the song:
0  12.5  A
12.5 20 B

Do you say that getting the boundary at 1.2 for the first file should be counted as a hit, while getting the boundary at 0.0 for the second file should be discarded as trivial? And do you say that every boundary detector should output a boundary at 0.0 for the first song? Note that the same holds for the endings of songs.
I would argue that a predicted boundary at 0.0 for the first song should be a false positive, a boundary at 1.2 for the first song should be a true positive, and a boundary at 0.0 for the second song should be a true positive.
Alternatively, the boundaries at 1.2 for the first song and at 0.0 for the second song should be ignored as being trivial.
Ignoring 0.0 for both the first and the second song is inconsistent.

> Yes, `mir_eval` as a philosophy tries to avoid dealing with annotation issues

This is not an annotation issue. Some files do start or end with silence while others don't, and the evaluation should be able to take this into account.

A possible solution would be for us to preprocess the SALAMI ground truth to discard "Silence" segments at the beginning and end and set `trim=False`, but from what I've seen, then the evaluation goes wrong because it uses the minimum and maximum of the ground truth to trim or pad the predictions.

I'm not sure about the approach here.  The clicks are coming in because the additive synthesis is catching the synthesized wave at nonzero values.  Ramping does fix it, but it seems to me like it'll be slow, and more complex than it needs to be.

Just my $2e-2, but I think it would be both cleaner and faster to bisect the wave position back (or forward) to the closest zero.  Since we only actually synthesize a few cycles of the wave and then do in-place tiling, it's actually pretty quick to compute the location of zeros.

Given the zeros, we have code for zc-bisection in [amen](https://github.com/algorithmic-music-exploration/amen/blob/master/amen/timing.py#L45-L73), but I suspect it's simpler to rewrite it if that's how you want to go.

It took me a while to find the time for this, but, I looked at the `auto_import_modules` issue today, and it seems that it's only used once in jedi's code, in `_load_module`, which is only called from `_do_import` if a module file was found.

Running with debug enabled, I can see this:
````
dbg: Parsed None, with 1 parsers in 1 splits.
dbg: start: 'Gtk' in <fast.FastModule: @1-2>
dbg: eval_element <Name: Gtk@2,4>@(2, 4)
dbg: finder.filter_name "<Name: Gtk@2,4>" in (<ModuleWrapper: <fast.FastModule: @1-2>>): '[<Name: Gtk@1,26>]'@(2, 4)
dbg: search_module 'gi' in None
dbg: pickle loaded: '/usr/lib64/python3.4/site-packages/gi/__init__.py'
dbg: search_module 'gi.repository' in paths ['/usr/lib64/python3.4/site-packages/gi']
dbg: pickle loaded: '/usr/lib64/python3.4/site-packages/gi/repository/__init__.py'

warning: None:1:26: E3 Potential ImportError.

It looks for the file, and since it doesn't find it, it raises an exception and `_load_module` is never called, which means the auto_import_modules setting is never taken into consideration in this case.

It would be nice to get the counts for sockets (cpus), cores and threads. But I don't think a simple count() will do. Potentially you can have multiple cpus with different core and thread counts. And some cores or threads can be powered down.

So maybe it would make sense (under linux) to parse /proc/cpuinfo and/or /sys/bus/cpu/devices/cpu*/topology/* and generate a tree-ish structure. Top level would be the sockets (physical_package_id), next level the cores and the threads as leaves. The top level would have cpu, core and thread counts. The lower levels would have more specific counts. E.g. cpu_tree()[0][2].thread_count() would be the number of threads on core 2 in physical package 0.

At that point it would be nice to include the cpu frequency as well. But maybe that's better left to another issue.

This one seemed pretty curious so I took a look as well. RE_DATE3 was matching `March 14th 8` as the date leaving `:30` for something else to parse. It was being a bit too greedy, the 8 was parsing as a day of the month in the same way that 14th was.

I think that the best way to avoid consuming the time here is to assert that what we think is the day of the month is not followed by a colon, am, or pm. Those are localized things so while the original regex included `am|pm` it's best to use the locale
```python
self.RE_DATE3 = r'''(?P<date>
                        (?:
                            (?:^|\s)
                            (?P<mthname>
                                {months}|{shortmonths}
                            )\b
                            |
                            (?:^|\s)
                            (?P<day>[1-9]|[012]\d|3[01])
                            (?P<suffix>{daysuffix}|)
                            (?!{timeseperator}|{meridian})\b
                            |
                            (?:,\s|\s)
                            (?P<year>\d\d(?:\d\d|))
                            (?!{timeseperator}|{meridian})\b
                        ){{1,3}}
                        (?(mthname)|$-^)
                    )'''.format(**self.locale.re_values)
```

The main difference is that the assertion `(?!{timeseperator}|{meridian})\b` is used rather than the original `(?!\d|am|pm)`. It has also been added after the year since 2-digit times will match the year regex.

with relevant sensitive strings replaced with <placeholder>

I've found that the row containing the large column does not get received in the result set and another call to fetchall() must be made on the cursor in order to receive any row proceeding the dropped row. This is completely silent.

note that using the same ODBC driver and the sqlcmd utility, we are able to use the -y flag to retrieve the full column:
`sqlcmd -y0 -S<sql_host> -U<user> -P<passwd> -itest_odbc.sql -otest_odbc_out.txt `
where test_odbc.sql can simply contain:

If you didn't click 'yes' to 'Proceed with updating your library' after the first download finished, then it (surprise!) hasn't updated your library yet. :) That's why you only see the new chapter afterwards.

Why does 'overwrite always' get the new chapter and 'overwrite if newer' doesn't think it needs to run?  'Overwrite if newer' compares the timestamp on the ebook file vs the reported date/time from the site.  But timezones can and will cause problems with that--timezones in Python are a headache that I'm not going to deal with.

Instead of 'overwrite if newer', use the 'update if new chapters' setting.  Not only will that compare chapter numbers instead of dates, it will reuse the chapters you've already downloaded for better performance.

For ECDH, the you just need to decode the the certificate and pass the `subjectPublicKey` as `Attribute.EC_POINT`. PKCS#11 expects this valid X9.62 DER encoded (I think they just gave up on adding parameters when they got to EC). See http://python-pkcs11.readthedocs.io/en/latest/applied.html#elliptic-curve.

When deriving shared keys, the other user's EC_POINT (still X9.62 DER encoded) is passed as a mechanism parameter. See http://python-pkcs11.readthedocs.io/en/latest/applied.html#ec-diffie-hellman

```bash
if [[ ~/.local/share/cheat/ ]]; then
  export CHEATCOLORS=1
  export DEFAULT_CHEAT_DIR=~/.local/share/cheat
fi
```

One other thing. The reversion has reintroduced an issue. If you submit the form via ajax with no inputs selected for 'Notify by', the rejected error message is correctly displayed.

If you then select a checkbox, the error message disappears, but the valid tick never re-appears, as the parent `ngForm` needs to have its 'rejected' error and `$message` cleared.

So at line 155, below

```formCtrl.$setValidity('required', valid);```

you need to add
```
formCtrl.$setValidity('rejected', true);
formCtrl.$message = '';
```

@heyquentin, I recommend looping over the *entries* instead of attempting to loop over the entry *indeces*:

Bad:

```
for i in range(len(d.entries)):
    # do something with `d.entries[i]`
```

Good:

```
for entry in d.entries:
    # do something with `entry`
```

Our team came to the same issue as @TZanke
We have ajax functions that dynamically load content which may or may not contain django-selectable widgets. All the other widgets that may be loaded dynamically are self initializing (using inline javascript or ajaxComplete bindings) and do not require any actions in our generic ajax callbacks. Your approach would cause changes in this generic ajax functions each time a developer uses a new widget that requires initialization.

In my opinion implicit and explicit binding should not be mixed since the reason for initialization is always the same: HTML content was loaded. Whether loaded with the first page load or whether loaded dynamically shouldn't matter.

Hi, which web framework are you using? I will assume that you are already sending an HTML message as part of your application (open tracking pixel does not work with plain text message). There are thus two major steps: adding the image tag in your email, and serving the pixel with a web server. # Step 1 - Adding a ```` tag First, you need to add a ```` tag, usually at the bottom of the email if it isn't too long. If the email is likely to be cut by the email reader because it is lengthy, you should put the pixel at the top of the ```` tag, but this may mess with your layout. The src attribute will be a link generated by [pytracking.get_open_tracking_url](https://github.com/resulto-admin/pytracking#get-open-tracking-link). You must make sure that you are able to receive requests at the path specified in the parameters. In the example, a webserver is able to receive GET requests at ``https://trackingdomain.com/path/`` Is this part ok? # Step 2 - Serving the pixel with a web server The second part is actually serving the pixel when the user opens the email. If you use Django, we already offer a View class. Otherwise, you need to do two things: 1. Decode the URL and record the open (e.g., by writing something to a log file or a database). 2. Serve the Pixel with the correct MIME type. About step 1, you need to call [pytracking.get_open_tracking_result](https://github.com/resulto-admin/pytracking#get-open-tracking-data-from-url). This will give you all the metadata you encoded with your link such as customer id. About step 2, you can serve your own 1x1 pixel saved on disk, or you can use pytracking which already has the pixel in memory by calling [pytracking.get_open_tracking_pixel](https://github.com/resulto-admin/pytracking#get-a-1x1-transparent-png-pixel) # Additional tips If you use Django, please [look at this section](https://github.com/resulto-admin/pytracking#using-pytracking-with-django) for more information about the View class we provide to serve an open and click tracking link. If you do not want to add the ```` src manually, you can use [pytracking.html.adapt_html](https://github.com/resulto-admin/pytracking#modifying-html-emails-to-add-tracking-links) which accepts your HTML as a string and outputs the same HTML with all links converted to click tracking links (optional) and adds an ```` tag for open tracking at the end of the email.

The dct function only works in one direction. According to the blog I based this off, I think they wanted a 2d DCT, so the function has to be called twice. 

As I understand it, there is no substantial theory behind any of the hash functions, people just come up with methods that seem to work for them. There are some other algorithms implemented here http://www.cs.huji.ac.il/~yweiss/SpectralHashing/ http://www.stonehenge.com/merlyn/LinuxMag/col50.html http://phash.org/

It would be interesting to test a bit more systematically the different hashes (and their parameters, e.g. hash size) -- but I am not sure how one would define one hash method to work better than another hash method. 

One approach could be taking a very large sample and finding the union of hash collisions across multiple methods, then classifying these manually as different or same. Then, the fraction of true and false positive hash collisions of each algorithm can be evaluated.

Another option would be to define various transforms (shifts, crops, rotations, watermarking, adding frames, hue-saturation-brightness changes, various instagram filters) and measure the amount of transformation until the hash changes.

I'm using django-pyodbc-azure on Yosemite.  Here's what works for me...

Use [homebrew] (http://brew.sh) to install unixodbc ("brew install unixodbc") and then install freetds with unixodbc support ("brew install freetds --with-unixodbc").  You'll probably want version 0.95 or later of freetds because it has support for SQL Server 2008 data types like "date" and "time".

Then, associate the driver name "FreeTDS" to the .so library by editing /usr/local/etc/odbcinst.ini and adding the following:

```ini
[FreeTDS]
Description=FreeTDS Driver
Driver=/usr/local/lib/libtdsodbc.so
Setup=/usr/local/lib/libtdsodbc.so
FileUsage=1
```

Next, make sure your project has the appropriate version of django-pyodbc-azure for your desired Django version.  Installing django-pyodbc-azure should also bring in pyodbc.

Your DATABASES dictionary in settings.py could then look like this:

```python
DATABASES = {
    'default': {
        'ENGINE': 'sql_server.pyodbc',
        'NAME': 'my_db_name',
        'USER': 'my_db_user',
        'PASSWORD': 'my_db_password',
        'HOST': 'myhost.company.com',
        'PORT': '1433',
        'OPTIONS': {
            'driver': 'FreeTDS',
            'host_is_server': True,
            'extra_params': 'TDS_VERSION=7.3',  # TDS 7.3 in freetds supports latest SQL Server 2008 types.
        }
    }
}
```

Tweak your DATABASES dictionary as needed for your environment.

__Note:__ Given the above setup, I had to work around a problem in our app that I describe in issue #37.

It looks like the issue here is that Python 2.7 always uses os.fork() in to implement multiprocessing.Process.start(). In Python3, there is a way to use other possible start methods which are 'fork', 'spawn' and 'forkserver'. You can set context as follows:
`multiprocessing.set_start_method('spawn')` to avoid the issues over fork-safety.

Ran following code snippet multiple times in Python3 successfully.

```
import multiprocessing as multiprocess
import subprocess
import mxnet as mx

def handle_task():
    ctx = mx.cpu()
    for _ in range(500):
        print('g')
        mx.nd.ones((2, 3), ctx)
        print('i')
        subprocess.check_output(["ls"])
        print('d')


if __name__ == '__main__':
    multiprocess.set_start_method('spawn', force=True)
    child_processes = [multiprocess.Process(target=handle_task, args=()) for _ in range(4)]

    for i in range(4):
        child_processes[i].start()

    for i in range(4):
        child_processes[i].join()
```

Importing any operator from `airflow.operators` is being deprecated because of creeping complexity. You should be seeing a warning like this:

```python

In [1]: import airflow
[2017-03-15 21:05:33,021] {__init__.py:56} INFO - Using executor LocalExecutor

In [2]: from airflow.operators import SubDagOperator
/Users/jlowin/git/airflow/airflow/utils/helpers.py:406: DeprecationWarning: Importing SubDagOperator directly from <module 'airflow.operators' from '/Users/jlowin/git/airflow/airflow/operators/__init__.py'> has been deprecated. Please import from '<module 'airflow.operators' from '/Users/jlowin/git/airflow/airflow/operators/__init__.py'>.[operator_module]' instead. Support for direct imports will be dropped entirely in Airflow 2.0.
  DeprecationWarning)
```

(looks like the module names got a little mangled at some point!)

So after thinking about this some more, I gave you some bad advice. You're caught in a tight place -- trying to adjust some deprecated behavior that's nonetheless maintained for backwards-compatibility reasons - so we need to awkwardly support both modes. 

Sorry to ask you to revise one more time, but I think the right way to do this is to leave the existing check and add an additional `or isinstance(task, SubDagOperator)` to the check, where `SubDagOperator` is properly imported as you're doing now. This way the legacy check is maintained for backwards compatibility and your way handles it properly going forward (and bonus points for putting a `#TODO remove in Airflow 2.0` over the legacy part of the check.)

Generally, the steps will probably look like:

1. Retrieving the records (OAI-PMH, FTP, FS, REST)
2. Converting each record from source format to intermediate format (lxml, dojson)
3. Apply wanted transformations on raw intermediate format to "enriched" intermediate format and return to client.

Even though knowledge of Invenio could give us access to a bunch of goodies, I suggest having Harvesting Kit provide useful utilities and common operations as a "leaf package". Then the clients (overlays or custom invenio-modules) decide the flow of things, pass parameters and make use of what Harvesting Kit offers. By using plugins (or contribs) as @Dziolas suggests we could also share the code to harvest and convert a certain feed. For example:

In your instance overlay:
```python
# overlay/config.py

APS_URL = "http://example.org"

# overlay/tasks: (e.g. scheduled periodically in Celery Beat on the server)

# this function can be generalised (import_string etc. and celery args)
@celery.task
def harvest_aps(workflow, *args, ...):   
    from invenio_workflows.api import start_delayed
    from harvestingkit.contrib.aps import harvest, convert

    last_harvested = # some way to retrieve last harvested date

    for harvested_record, harvested_files in harvest(
            url=cfg.get("APS_URL"),
            from=last_harvested_date,
        ):
        clean_record_dict = convert(harvested_record)
        payload = {
            "files": harvested_files,
            "record": clean_record_dict
        }
        start_delayed(workflow, data=[payload])
        # now your very own ingestion workflow takes over (processing)
```

Then in Harvesting Kit:
```python
# harvestingkit/contrib/aps/__init__.py

from .getter import harvest
from .converter import convert

__all__ = ["harvest", "convert"]


# harvestingkit/contrib/aps/getter.py

def harvest(url, from, until, ...):
    # implement core retrieval logic (could be spread over several files)
    yield record, files


# harvestingkit/contrib/aps/convert.py

def convert(some_record):
    # implement conversion logic (could be classes etc.)
    return cleaned_record
```

The main body of harvesting kit could then be solely for common utilities:
```
harvestingkit/ftp.py : contains ftp retrieval functions
harvestingkit/rest.py : contains REST retrieval functions
harvestingkit/extraction.py : contains unzip/untar functions
harvestingkit/text.py : contains text manipulation functions
harvestingkit/xml.py : contains xml manipulation functions
harvestingkit/sanitize.py : contains common sanitization functions
harvestingkit/jats.py : common extraction operations related to JATS formats
etc.
```

It's a slightly different take on the initial idea, but what do you think?

On careful thought this seems like a reasonable thing to do. Cargo (of Rust) has something similar called [Workspace](https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/second-edition/ch14-03-cargo-workspaces.html). A workspace Cargo.toml does not have a set of dependencies itself, but only references a collection of child projects (members). Each member’s Cargo.toml represents a project on its own, and has its own lock file.

Note that a workspace in Cargo is all-or-nothing. A workspace Cargo.toml cannot specify any dependencies, and a non-workspace Cargo.toml cannot have any children. It would be worth discussing whether Pipfile should also be like this, or if we’d allow both `[include]` and `[packages]` to work in the same file.

As for the command-line API, I’d say it’s better to have a seperate subcommand, `pipenv include [subproject-to-include]`. Also it would make sense to be able to include another Pipfile, as well as a requirements.txt file.

@harshavardhana 

> This will fail on windows. We need to explicitly close the file. Can you validate ?

No it should not. The `with` statement was introduced a long time back (in Py 2.5 https://docs.python.org/3/whatsnew/2.6.html#pep-343-the-with-statement and more powerful in py 3 - https://docs.python.org/3/reference/compound_stmts.html#with ) and is meant to do things like close files that is alternatively done with same guarantee with `try...catch...finally` block (just calling `f.close()` after the with statement would not have the same effect - in case there was a bug in windows - because of exceptions).

I tried to find if there are any bug reports related to Windows and closing files and the with statement, but I didn't see any. If there was such a bug, it would be quite serious and there would be multiple reports.

> Why a strict bytes type ?

In the case of `fput_object` this is not a problem, since our code opens the file in binary mode and this returns only bytes.

In `put_object`, the data returned by the `io.RawIOBase` instance, could return even str (unicode) streams, as they are user provided. How the unicode stream should be stored on disk requires us to know what encoding is intended by the user - it is not necessarily `utf-8` (user may intend utf-16, etc). To avoid any unintended encoding of file-data we accept only bytes - this makes sure that the user encodes it to bytes and then calls `put_object`.

In other situations it is ok for us to encode str type to utf-8 and convert to bytes, as in the library, we know what data we are encoding in all other situations.

> By the way is tab forbidden ONLY in 1.1 in Yaml? 

> 

Hi dervus; 
I disagree with your comment
check yaml spec 1.2 ,[ section 5.5-white space charecters](http://www.yaml.org/spec/1.2/spec.html#id2775170)
in 1.2 spec, **tab** and space are considered whitespace characters and CAN be used for indentation.

Quoting from [section: 6.2. Separation Spaces](http://www.yaml.org/spec/1.2/spec.html#id2778241):
> Outside indentation and scalar content, YAML uses white space characters for separation between tokens within a line. Note that such white space may safely include tab characters

> 

further more, quoting from [section: 6.1 Indentation Spaces](http://www.yaml.org/spec/1.2/spec.html#id2777534):

> In YAML block styles, structure is determined by indentation. In general, indentation is defined as a zero or more space characters at the start of a line

> 

Yaml spec 1.2 direct the "space characters" from the quote above  to section 5.5 - defining these spaces as  **tabs**  and spaces.

In contrast to spec 1.2, spec 1.1 state the opposite to tab characters:
check yaml spec 1.1, [section 5.5. Miscellaneous Characters](http://yaml.org/spec/1.1/#id871856):

> An ignored space character outside scalar content. Such spaces are used for indentation and separation between tokens. To maintain portability, **tab characters must not be used** in these cases..

> 

Spec 1.1 also gives example 5.12 with code using tab which stated as Error. The same example is use in spec 1.2 section 5.5 - and in it is OK.
You can also check [section 6.3 (Separation spaces)](http://yaml.org/spec/1.1/#separation space/) in yaml spec 1.1

David, you need to understand what the different components do before you can make a judgement to remove them or not.

TP and TP10X2 *cannot* be combined. The former is a mostly-Python implementation and the latter is an entirely C++ implementation. They are functionally identical (we have explicit tests to enforce this) but the mostly-Python version is easier to experiment with while the C++ one is faster / better for production.

And similarly, fast_temporal_memory contains a subclass of the new temporal memory that uses a fast C++ data structure. So if we had a completely-C++ implementation of the temporal memory then yes, we could swap from the hybrid Python class to the bindings for the complete C++ implementation.

The general philosophy is that we want a full C++ implementation and a full Python implementation with tests to ensure they are functionally identical. This allows for easy experimentation in Python with fast C++ implementations for production use. But sometimes we deviate from this when we can move small pieces of the Python to C++ for a significant speed up while still having most of the code in Python for easy experimentation. It seems like the different implementations and strategies make you uncomfortable but we aren't going to artificially restrict ourselves to a single implementation or to only pure Python or pure C++ implementations.

OK, I just pushed a commit (https://github.com/GalSim-developers/GalSim/commit/3326e634a0f8bf31ca2e45d7d010e8dbae9353d3)  that addressed two things with the radius and flux cuts:

- I had been defining the radius using the re-scaled (by factor of 0.6) radius, since in the GREAT3 code that's how we defined the radius.  But in the simulations produced by cgc.yaml, it's using the actual radius and flux from the parametric fits for the cuts.  And hence we should be using the un-rescaled radii for the cuts in cgc.yaml.  (You had previously undone the rescaling in cgc.yaml, so our new results should agree, but I just wanted to check using the pre-tabulated radii that this was true.)

- I had a bug in the flux definition in the original version of the script.  I defined it in a way that un-did the size-rescaling in one place but not the other, creating a sersic n-dependent bug in the flux.

I updated the script that produced those cuts to simply take the new version of the parametric fits catalog, with precomputed fluxes and radii, and produce suggested cuts.  Its new output is:

    ...or, a fraction 0.951254 of SNR failures at the high end
Beginning work for FWHM=0.80 arcsec:
    Radius cut at 0.358 arcsec eliminates a fraction 0.791259 of res failures
    Flux cut at 63.975 eliminates a fraction 0.496683 of SNR failures
    ...or, a fraction 0.963319 of SNR failures at the high end
Beginning work for FWHM=0.95 arcsec:
    Radius cut at 0.404 arcsec eliminates a fraction 0.796609 of res failures
    Flux cut at 67.123 eliminates a fraction 0.451446 of SNR failures
    ...or, a fraction 0.972320 of SNR failures at the high end
```

So for FWHM = (0.5, 0.65, 0.8, 0.95), the ```min_hlr``` based on the COSMOS catalog values should be (0.263, 0.311, 0.358, 0.404) arcsec and ```max_flux``` should be (68.408, 64.735, 63.975, 67.123).

The results for ```min_hlr``` are the identical to current results, confirming that we handled all the fudge factors correctly before.

The results for ```max_flux``` are, as hoped, higher than before.  So we will exclude fewer objects in the pre-selection (indeed we were definitely excluding plenty of reasonable objects) and the stamp rejection should kick in.  A decent quadratic model for this is 
```
        max_flux : '$63.9 + 75.8*(psf_fwhm-0.76)**2'
```

My customers prefer the feature the way it's currently implemented. Losing transactions is viewed as annoyance that is better avoided, but is a risk they are willing to take to stay available. This is coincidentally the same way that Pacemaker's pgsql resource agent does sync replication. I can see that some people might have a different view of the durability-availability tradeoff. It's almost possible to achieve that today - just set synchronous_standby_names manually on each node. The only small issue is that with more than 2 nodes and 2 simultaneous failures it's not possible to be sure that it's actually safe to fail over because the second one of those failures might have been the synchronous standby at the time. I didn't add a mode switch for this because I didn't have a real world usecase to design against. If you say you would need this and are willing to help design the "extra durability" mode, I'm happy to add it.

My other tests also indicate that this is either a feature or a bug of the Python 3 memory manager. This behavior can be reproduced without pympler by just using `sys.getsizeof(f.__dict__)`:

```
>>> sys.getsizeof(f.__dict__)
10992
>>> f.__dict__
{}
```

Interestingly this size keeps showing on copies of the dict as well:

```
>>> sys.getsizeof(f.__dict__.copy())
10992
```

I believe the question for us is whether Python truly allocates this much memory for the instances. If it does, the profiling is correct. If it doesn't, this would be a bug in the implementation of CPython's `sys.getsizeof`.

Four things:

a) The `__slots__` attribute only shows the size of the tuple-like `__slots__` space, like `asizeof` did before ... by creating a tuple on the fly.

b) To size `__slots__` only once would require using the exact sample tuple (with the same `id`) for all instances of a class.  That could easily be done by using the class-level `__slots__` instead of an on-the-fly tuple.

c) However, the `__slots__` size will be included in the total size of the first instance of a class, effectively _-and incorrectly-_ counting that size twice.  Avoiding the double count would require gymnastics across `asizeof`, which is already too large, too complex.

d) `__slots__` are a class-level attribute.  Therefore, the proper place to report the size of tuple-like `__slots__` storage is when sizing the class, not the instance.

`google.api_core.exceptions._from_http_response` tries hard to preserve any error message or errors from the response's payload.  The issue here is that there *are* no errors:  a [206 response](https://www.w3.org/Protocols/rfc2616/rfc2616-sec10.html#sec10.2.7) isn't an error.

If `google.resumable_media` is raising an `InvalidResponse` exception, ISTM that we should just let it propagate:  there is no point in rewrapping it.  @dhermes is the person who knew enough to argue otherwise, but he's on leave at the moment.

I think this is the same as cloudtools/awacs#15. Just ran into the same issue, using

```
Resource=[Join('', [awacs.s3.ARN(''), Ref(bucket), '/*'])],
```
worked for me. Note that `awacs.s3.ARN('')` produces `'arn:aws:s3:::'`. My first idea `awacs.s3.ARN(Ref(bucket))` will *not* work, as the function does not know what to do with a `troposhere.Ref` object.


Working example:

```
bucket = t.add_resource(
    s3.Bucket(
        'Bucket',
        BucketName=Join('-', [Ref(AWS_STACK_NAME), Ref(environment)]),
    )
)

# Bucket policy enforcing server-side encryption
bucket_policy = t.add_resource(
    s3.BucketPolicy(
        'BucketPolicy',
        Bucket=Ref(bucket),
        PolicyDocument=PolicyDocument(
            Id='EnforceServersideEncryption',
            Version='2012-10-17',
            Statement=[
                Statement(
                    Action=[awacs.s3.PutObject],
                    Condition=Condition(
                        StringNotEquals(
                            {
                                's3:x-amz-server-side-encryption': 'AES256'
                            }
                        )
                    ),
                    Effect=Deny,
                    Principal=Principal(Everybody),
                    Resource=[Join('', [awacs.s3.ARN(''), Ref(bucket), '/*'])],
                    Sid='DenyIncorrectEncryptionHeader'
                )
            ]
        )
    )
)
```
It's not a CloudFormation property, it's just AWS API - CloudFormation (surprisingly) doesn't cover 100% of the API.

Hi @eupharis, apologies, I'm not actively working on this project at the moment (other projects call as you will understand I'm sure), so it's taken a while to get to this.

Thanks for writing up the analysis page. This helps a lot in getting details ironed out before you go in to do the actual implementation. I've been surprised a bit to discover that the implementation generally turns out to be the easy part; it's the discovery, analysis, and resolution up front that take the time and all the noodling :)

The approach you suggest in the analysis page appears to be inconsistent with the existing approach for that sort of thing and also how Word handles the broader bit about headers and footers.

I just realized I had started some analysis work on headers/footers a while back. I just posted it here, you might want to have a look:
https://github.com/python-openxml/python-docx/commit/e8fb68e743046240aed0d6396ef9cb1381cde4b4

I think the first thing is that headers and footers have a natural affinity for sections, rather than the document as a whole. Most documents probably only have a single section, but of course the API needs to work for the general case. So the first step in the protocol would be something like this I'm pretty sure:

```python
>>> document = Document('xyx.docx')
>>> section = document.sections[-1]  #last section, for example
>>> odd_page_header = section.primary_header
>>> odd_page_header
<docx.header.Header object at 0x1029a6820>
>>>
```

Another item that jumps out is that there can be as many as three headers and/or footers for each section, one for odd pages (the primary header/footer), one for even pages (if laying out verso/recto for binding), and a possibly different first-page header/footer. So the API will need to both provide access to those and provide properties like `has_first_page_header` or whatever to allow discovery of what is defined.

I think you'll want to take a look at the MS API for headers and footers to see what they do. We usually find it pretty useful to model after that API, although there are important departures in certain cases to make things more Pythonic or just more rational. In any case it's useful to understand the MS API protocol as a key input to the analysis. I know in this case they maintain a collection accessed by indices 1, 2, or 3. I'm thinking we just make three properties (six actually when you include footers, e.g. `.even_page_header` that either return the appropriate section or None if one is not defined.

You'll also want to pull together the key parts of the XML schema along with examples/snippets. You can find plenty of examples of those in the other analysis pages. You might be able to reuse some of the bits from sections here, since they're so closely related:
https://github.com/eupharis/python-docx/blob/master/docs/dev/analysis/features/sections.rst

Also have a look at this issue for some ideas and prior work:
https://github.com/python-openxml/python-docx/issues/104

Let us know how you go :)

> I have put a recompiled version of the jpmml-sklearn jar inside my resources directory.

For documentation purposes, these are the steps for updating the JPMML-SkLearn dependency of the SkLearn2PMML package:

1. Update JPMML-SkLearn version in `pom.xml`. When working with locally tweaked JPMML-SkLearn library, then the version identifier is typically `1.3-SNAPSHOT`.
2. Manually delete all JAR files from the `/sklearn2pmml/resources` directory (only `__init__.py` should remain).
3. Execute Apache Maven build `mvn clean package`. This will download the JPMML-SkLearn library, and all its transitive dependencies into the `/sklearn2pmml/resources` directory.
4. Optionally, update the version identifier in `sklearn2pmml/metadata.py`. For example, introduce some sort of suffix to signal that this is a "tweaked" version, not an official version.

Hi, this is already possible:
You can use the time range facet to choose your time filter and then the badge facet to choose the badge.

In a query string (for both the search interface and the API), this will look like: `datasets/?badge=my-badge&temporal_coverage=2010-01-01-2015-12-31`

You can to do the same thing with tags: `datasets/?tag=tag-1&tag=tag-2&temporal_coverage=2010-01-01-2015-12-31`

In fact you can combine any filterable facet and also use the `OR` operator (`|`), so to list datasets with data between **2010/01/01** and **2015/12/31** having **my-badge** badge and having **tag-1** or **tag-2** tag you can write:
```
datasets/?badge=my-badge&tag=tag-1|tag-2&temporal_coverage=2010-01-01-2015-12-31
```

You cannot mix packages from `conda-forge` and `conda-defaults` because they are not binary compatible: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Binary_code_compatibility

Please remove `conda-forge` from your channels then do `conda update --all` then ensure `conda-forge` does not appear anywhere in the full output from `conda list --show-channel-urls`

I can also confirm this is repeatable on [Travis-CI](https://travis-ci.org/choderalab/yank/jobs/259747263#L575-L634), but it appears to be a problem when searching the channels missing  noarch directory for packages since conda packages are still installable in general, but none from conda-forge or others are found.

Example: [build from yesterday](https://travis-ci.org/choderalab/yank/jobs/259431184#L501-L521) correctly pulls the packages from the higher priority conda-forge channel, but [fails this morning and falls back to default channel](https://travis-ci.org/choderalab/yank/jobs/259747263#L501-L544). Comparison is on same Travis-build version and same base conda install version, so there should not be any difference.

When it tries to find a package on channels without a `noarch` directory, it [appears to fail](https://travis-ci.org/choderalab/yank/jobs/259747263#L575-L634). In this case there should be a `nose-timer` on `conda-forge` and the `omnia` channel, but neither are fetched and the install fails since it does not exist on `default`. Since the package `nose-timer` *should* exist on two channels in the search, it should find at least one of them, but does not.

The contents of `activate.d` and `deactivate.d` scripts is really a packaging issue.  I think over time we've been doing better with proper quoting in these shell scripts.

In general, issue https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/6820 should give conda more control over stuff like this.  So that we can fix these problems with a code change to conda rather than changes that need to be made across a bunch of already-built packages.

> Is there a way to use conda whereby it would not use hard links in such a way?

Yes.  Either use the `--copy` CLI flag with `create`, `install`, and `update` operations, or do

    conda config --set always_copy true

or

    conda config --system --set always_copy true

> but I feel like I may be driven away if it can't be made more compatible with pip.

Technically, it's if pip can't be made more compatible with conda.  It really is pip that's the bad actor in this situation.  It's on our list to patch pip to behave better with conda, and apply that patch to the pip you get as the conda package (and the one we always install).  We'll also be submitting that patch upstream.

I spoke with @asottile a little about how we might resolve the issues on this branch and get it shipped. There's a couple minor things to resolve, but I think the main design questions are:

**To reduce the need to map file extensions to file tags:**

We can use the `mimetypes` module. This will get us a mimetype like `text/x-python` which we can use some logic to try to simplify (e.g. removing the `<type>/` and `x-`). It might be a bit more complicated than that (especially for non-`text/`), but I think we can do a pretty good job here.

There are also some more complicated ones, e.g. `.c` is `text/x-csrc`.

The bigger concern will be things that `mimetypes` can't identify. There are some common extensions like `.go` that python3.5 stdlib doesn't seem to identify. Because of this, I think we're still going to have to maintain a mapping of extensions to tags for some extensions (or use something besides `mimetypes`).

We might also have a problem here where newer versions of stdlib can identify some extensions that older ones can't?


**To reduce the need to map shebang interpreters to file tags:**

This one we can't use `mimetypes` for, but we can probably use some logic to do the right thing in most cases. For example, if we strip off things that look like versions, that would take care of `python2.7 => python`.

There's still a couple special cases like `('^(ba|da|tc|[ckz])?sh$', ['shell']),` and `('^node(js)?$', ['javascript']),`. Are those important enough to still maintain a mapping? Not sure.

> If each of this would point to another git repository installation would become a real issue.

I'm not convinced of this -- I have several thousand git repos cloned on my machine and it's not currently an issue.  `pre-commit` also is smart about sharing these in a central cache (it's not an NxN problem, just an N problem).

> I am in favour of having hooks defined in a centralized repository (registry?)

A good portion of the design is intentionally away from centralized control -- because it is entirely done through `git` it makes it very easy for users to pick and choose, modify in site-specific ways, or even run entirely off the grid with private repositories.  I don't see this changing, it's one of the core design decisions.  The closest to a "central registry" is the `hooks.html` page (and associated `json` file) -- yes the discoverability and such could be better here but for now it mostly serves its purpose.

> Hook dependencies should be installed per hook instead of per repository.

This would actually make the disk space problem much much worse -- in almost all cases the installed environment's disk usage dwarfs the repository size.

> There is also one thing that concerns me a lot, security

I mean, if you find a repository you don't want to use just remove it from your configuration :P.  That said I do at some point want to add a hardening option (sha1 locking) but I haven't found a sound design for it that isn't just a merge-conflict-by-design.

Sure it isn't

> do we really need something like WSGI?

We absolutely need a clearly specified server/application interface layer, yes.

Suppose you're a python developer and you've got a great new idea for a web framework, what's your starting point at the moment? Implement the protocol, choose and integrate an http parser, figure out how to get flow control right (in both directions), add ssl contexts and signal handling, design a messaging interface that abstracts those details away from the rest of the codebase, add request logging, figure out clean shutdown etc.etc.etc.

Equally consider existing WSGI based Python web frameworks that are considering evolving to support asyncio deployment modes. Right now they'd either have to understand and implement all of those different aspects, and clone a large amount of existing implementation code across from one of the existing asyncio frameworks.

Have a standard interface would also allow use to have shared middleware, eg. equivalents to werkzeug's debug WSGI middleware or whitenoise's static file serving WSGI middleware.

> increases already high learning curve for asyncio projects.

I'd argue the opposite. By getting the abstractions right, we remove a huge number of details that developers would otherwise need to get their heads around.

Consider a hello world application, with the proposed interface...

    async def app(message, channels):
        await channels['reply'].send({
            'status': 200,
            'headers': [
                [b'content-type', b'text/plain'],
            ],
            'content': b'Hello, world'
        })

Without having some kind of standardized interface at that level we're instead telling developers "implementing a server is easy, just subclass asyncio.Protocol, implement data_recieved, hook up an http parser, and you're good to go" (*)

(*) vastly simplified.

> introduces new abstraction layer

Even if you *don't* have a tightly specified server/application interface, you *still* end up having an interface boundary between the two. It's just that it ends up being different in each web framework implementation. It'll just be less thoroughly designed as a result, and not interoperable in any way.

> I don't see python as a first choice for new high-load projects nowadays.

The need for a clean interface layer stands whether or not that's true. However one of the reasons that I think this work is particularly important at the moment is because I think Python's on the cusp of being able to change that, and have an ecosystem of asyncio frameworks that are highly performance-competitive against the rest of the web landscape.

The down-side to having independent schemas is that there'll be a lot of duplication. But I think this can be mitigated with scripts. (Possibly several scripts, and possibly make targets too? We can start with just one or two simple scripts.) For example, we could have a script that scrapes an API, parses the JSON and spits out a basic schema file. That schema file would then need to be fleshed out by hand (the field types in particular would be a pain), but it's a start.

What would the schema look like? I'm thinking of something like this:

```python
schema = {
    'ActivationKey': {
        'fields': {
            'auto_attach': BooleanField(),
            'content_view': OneToOneField(ContentView),
            'description': StringField(),
            'environment': OneToOneField(LifecycleEnvironment),
            'host_collection': OneToManyField(HostCollection),
            'max_content_hosts': IntegerField(),
            'name': StringField(required=True),
            'organization': OneToOneField(Organization, required=True),
            'unlimited_content_hosts': BooleanField(),
        }
    },
    'Architecture': {…},
    …
```

If there's better approaches, of course, I'm very happy to hear them. One major problem with this approach is that we're working with raw Python code, and how in the world do you generate that? It may be better to make JSON schemas, or something else entirely.

.NET Core 2.0 doesn't work with 15.2. It requires 15.3 and later. There are two reasons for that:

- VS 15.0 (and .1 and .2) was hard-coded to use .NET Core 1.x. VS 15.3 includes an extensibility model for .NET Core so that VS is much more flexible to supporting .NET Core SDK versions (which in turn decides runtime versions).
-  Many VS improvements were added to make VS work better for .NET Core. These are more helpful than fully required, but it's also the scenario we tested, so it's important to use 15.3.

Or you can disable inotify at all, check out config.json file description in configuration reference.

I do not see what is the issue? Ever since 1.0 colorschemes consist of three parts (and this is described in the documentation): colorschemes/(name).json, colorschemes/(ext)/__main__.json and colorschemes/(ext)/(name).json, of which any file may be missing (but either the first or the last must be present). These files are merged with each other to form the resulting colorscheme.

So missing wm/default.json file is not the issue. The fact that without color definitions segment is not going to work is not either. Since you have not setup logging then disappearing error is neither (maybe it is an issue that this error is displayed *at all* **in the bar**). If you want to add missing group to the top-level colorscheme then you can file a PR, but first check that it is missing *in develop* (I do not remember when exactly regarding releases, but a number of highlight group locations were moved to top level).

Depends on how much data we are talking about. If your simple table is just a few numbers you can just return a list, dictionary or list of lists from the main function. That will be stored as the result of the experiment and is pretty easy to query. Alternatively, you could store it as part of the [info-dict](https://sacred.readthedocs.io/en/latest/observers.html?highlight=info#info-dict).

If your result consists of *a lot* of data it might be wise to *not* to store it in the main database entry so as to not slow queries down too much or to exceed the 16MB limit of MongoDB. In those cases using an Artifact is still your best option.

API wise, maybe something like this?

> Let me know if I made a mistake here
It really depends on usage scenario, so the best thing is to make measurements using 
the simple cloning technique as suggested above and then determine if the extra feature is worth it.
I assume that you are using the state in incremental mode, so the overhead of several incremental calls would dominate setup time for a single incremental call.

If it is worth to make things more advanced, then I would consider to go for something less than cloning everything: I would expose interfaces for extracting and adding internalized and learned clauses to the smt_context and have the new context re-internalize (but not simplify) the state so that we would not have to clone internal data-structures. It would not be an exact clone, but it would contain the state corresponding to how search has progressed. We would also have to add a "translate" facility to the solver class, but can use a default simple implementation.

It is an interesting and succinct example.
In a sense you are asking Z3 to establish a parser for a context free language. The model finding features of Z3 look for essentially finite models. These models are represented in a constructive way, so would have to encode such a parser. Z3 is therefore not sufficient to establish such structures.

> In fact, that would probably be better...I'm only explaining why it works the way it works now, not saying that is the best way.

It's not the best way.  Init should take care of creating schemas that don't exist.  I just hit this same bug on a project and having to solve it by clearing the DB first is really not ideal.  Detecting schemas that don't exist and creating them during init would be a much better solution.

> I am not sure of this case, but if there is a tie, that two variables have their Nth dimension to be 1, which one do we make broadcastable ?

All of them. Ideally, any variable that has a shape that we know to be `1` should be broadcastable in that dimension.

> But can't we ensure the broadcast pattern as and when the `MergeLayer` is called?

The constructor should check that the shapes match, but we should not store the broadcast pattern in an attribute to reuse it in `get_output_for`. This would suggest that it's something we can only compute in the constructor, but actually we can just as well compute it in `get_output_for`.

> Unfortunately, the `InputLayer` doesn't have `get_output_for` method.

Exactly. The reason was that the definition is `get_output_for(self, input, **kwargs)`, but the `InputLayer` does not process an `input`, it provides the first input. So we handle the input layers as special cases in `lasagne.layers.get_output`.

> I am also fine with adding the method to that class and having this functionality here

That's not really possible. We need to support `lasagne.layers.get_output(network, {somelayer: myvar})`, which assigns `myvar` as the output of `somelayer`. If we add a `get_output_for` method to an `InputLayer`, we still cannot call it when overriding its output with a custom variable. This would change the semantics to assigning `myvar` as the input of `somelayer`, which makes a huge difference if `somelayer` is anything else than an `InputLayer`.

> If there is a way by which we can have the same Variable, but with altered broadcast pattern, that would be perfect IMO.

There are three common usage patterns:
```python
network = InputLayer((1, 2, 3))
myvar = network.input_var
network = DenseLayer(network, 10)
output = lasagne.layers.get_output(network)
```
```python
myvar = T.tensor3('input')
network = InputLayer((1, 2, 3), myvar)
network = DenseLayer(network, 10)
output = lasagne.layers.get_output(network)
```
```python
network = InputLayer((1, 2, 3))
network = DenseLayer(network, 10)
myvar = T.tensor3('input')
output = lasagne.layers.get_output(network, myvar)
```
(Ignore the fact that this example doesn't actually require broadcastable input to the dense layer, I didn't want to create a more complex example.)
The first usage pattern is currently handled correctly by the InputLayer, since it creates the input variable that is used later. The second usage pattern could be handled by messing with the variable, but has the disadvantage that `network.input_layer.input_var is not myvar` afterwards. The third pattern can only be handled within `get_output`.

So I would suggest to add a helper function and prep the inputs when they're collected in `get_output`.

I've updated the cursor scheme to use shorter cursors – instead of dumping everything to JSON, then base64-encoding, I base64-encode each separate value as a string, remove the padding, then join with the unused `.` character.

In general, this leads to shorter cursors, as I can avoid encoding quotes for strings, and need only a single byte to encode dividers.

This format also opens up the possibility of directly encoding binary values to base64 instead of strings down the road.

I don't want independent flags for everyone who wants a handler. If you want a handler, put it in the list. That is where all the handlers go.

>> Your remaining two points seems to negate each other.

They don't, and here's why. The biggest concern is "do no unintended harm" to the user's code. There are already cases where you can add a custodian handler and it will do nothing. For example, if you set an AliasingErrorHandler but you also set NELECT, the AliasingErrorHandler basically just deactivates. The MeshSymmetryHandler also deactivates itself if ISYM=F or if auto_kpoint generation is used. This logic is there directly in the handler itself to prevent them from doing anything unusual, regardless of whether someone adds the handler through atomate or not.

If the DriftErrorHandler is really only valid/useful for EDIFFG < 0, it can have a similar logic to those other handlers. Then, if someone (not even using atomate) reuses the same handler set as their colleague but switches EDIFFG > 0, your handler shuts itself down gracefully.

No one is confused when a handler doesn't do anything, they are confused when they are unaware that VASP parameters are going to change from what they thought (e.g., set a null handler set to run a "raw" VASP run with custodian double relaxation feature, but still are getting error corrections from some handler that got added somewhere else in the code).

So the plan would be:
- DriftErrorHandler turns itself off if its added in teh context of an invalid parameter set, like other handlers that do the same. This works independent of atomate.
- DriftErrorHandler gets added to the handler_sets where it makes sense in atomate.
- (optional) you can add some code in RunVaspCustodian that removes DriftErrorHandler from the handler_set if it's present there and ediffg >= 0 if you want. This might make things a little more clear for someone reading only atomate code and also will prevent a little computation time from the DriftHandler checking to make sure it's valid when we are pretty confident it won't be. But again, the major issue is handlers sneaking in that you are unaware of.

@syrusakbary Hello, I know that subscriptions are going to be implemented in graphene soon but i just want to share how I managed to get them working with channels.

-  first I made a route for both http and ws 
```
  1                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                         
4   channel_routing = [                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     
  1             route('http.request', ws_GQLData, path=r"^/gql"),                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           
  2             route('websocket.connect', ws_GQL_connect, path=r"^/gql"),                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  
  3             route('websocket.receive', ws_GQLData, path=r"^/gql"),                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      
      ...                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                
  6         ]                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      
```

- than i accepted the connection
```
@allowed_hosts_only
def ws_GQL_connect(message):
    message.reply_channel.send({"accept": True})
 
```     
- parsed the request and executed the query
```
@channel_session                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            
def ws_GQLData(message):                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    
    clean = json.loads(message.content['text'])                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             
    try:                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    
        query = clean['query']                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              
    except:                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 
        query = None                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        
    try:                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    
        foovar = clean['variables']                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                         
    except:                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                 
        foovar = None                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       
    kwargs = {'context_value': message}                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     
    result = schema.execute(query, variable_values=foovar,  **kwargs)                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       
    message.reply_channel.send(                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             
        {                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   
            'text': str({'data': json.loads(json.dumps(result.data))})                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                      
        })          
```                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           
- Subscription class that adds user to Group
```
class ProductSubscritption(object):

    """test"""
    sub_product = graphene.Field(ProductType, description='subscribe to updated product', uuid=graphene.String())

    def resolve_sub_product(self, info, **args):
        uuid = args.get('uuid')
        qs = ProductType.get_queryset()
        try:
            Group('gqp.product-updated.{0}'.format(uuid)).add(info.context.reply_channel)
        except:
            pass
        return qs.get(uuid=uuid)
```

- post_save receiver that sends messages, Note: here the client only receives the uuid that was updated,
    than it is up to the client to do another query to get the specific fields they want from that object if any
```

@receiver(post_save, sender=Product)
def send_update(sender, instance, created, *args, **kwargs):
    uuid = str(instance.uuid)
    if created:
        Group("gqp.product-add").send({'added': True})
        return
    Group('gqp.product-updated.{0}'.format(uuid))\
        .send({'text': uuid})

```

Quick note on the `writable` flag: mutable accessors return non-const pointers and references, which can be used later on while the numpy array writability flag has changed. If there is no consistent way to support the mutability flag in C++, why not drop it completely?

So after thinking about this for a while, I think adding argument policies is not going to work: it would be a fairly intrusive change to all of pybind11 with (at least for now) only one purpose of avoiding copies that even Eigen itself doesn't avoid.

I've just pushed an added wrapper class to this PR that lets binding code that really wants to avoid copying to bind to an intermediate lambda that takes an object of special `Eigen::Ref` subclass called `py::EigenNoCopyRef` (which simply inherits from `Ref`, and takes the same template parameters).  The lambda then simply forwards the call to the `Ref`-taking function:

```C++
void func(const Eigen::Ref<const Eigen::MatrixXd> &mat) { /* ... */ }
m.def("func1", &func)
m.def("func2", [](const py::EigenNoCopyRef<const Eigen::MatrixXd> &mat) { return func(mat); })
```
Then when loading the argument to `func2()`, conversion fails if a copy would be required, but `func1()` would copy an incompatible matrix into a temporary.

That leaves you with behaviour that works more or less like Eigen by default, but still gives you a way to say "don't copy this!".

Detecting this isn't too difficult: `std::is_base_of<Base, Derived>` will be true but `std::is_convertible<D*, B*>` will be false.  (If you just have the member function pointer, you'll have to deduce `B` first like pybind's `method_adaptor`).

> > Sure, but looping is still something that people will want to do reasonably fast. Checking attributes at every iteration is a good way to kill performance.
> But computing byte offset from scratch on every iteration is not optimal regardless. If you have a tight loop, the chances are you'll fetch a pointer once anyway and then increment the strides manually in the loops to avoid needless multiplications and stride fetching? NumPy C API has some macros (PyArray_ITER_*) to simplify iteration, maybe instead of removing bound checks we could provide iteration primitives?

I agree, xtensor has efficient iterator pairs that allow people to iterate more efficiently on arrays, but we should expect people to write quick-&-easy things with loops over indices.

For the record, `certbot-auto` can be made to work on CentOS 5.  To get set up, do the following (some of these steps may be unnecessary, depending on what you have already done in the past):

```
rm -rf ~/.local/share/letsencrypt/
yum install epel-release
yum install python26 python26-devel openssl101e openssl101e-devel
wget https://dl.eff.org/certbot-auto
chmod a+x certbot-auto
```
You will also need to edit `/etc/httpd/conf.d/ssl.conf` and remove or comment-out the `VirtualHost` section in it.

Then use the following command whenever you want to invoke `certbot-auto` (if your system is 32 bit, then substitute `lib` for `lib64`):
```
LE_PYTHON=python26 CFLAGS="-I/usr/include/openssl101e" LDFLAGS="-L/usr/lib64/openssl101e" ./certbot-auto -t -a webroot -i apache
```
The `CFLAGS` and `LDFLAGS`  environment variables are only used when `certbot-auto` upgrades itself to a newer version, but since you don't know when that might happen, they need to always be in the environment.

Text mode (`-t`) is needed because the curses UI makes use of a `dselect` dialog box which only became available with dialog 1.1 (CentOS 5 has dialog 1.0).  `webroot` authentication must be used because the installed httpd and its mod_ssl don't support SNI.

It is possible to use multiple name-based virtual hosts on a single port, so long as you are willing to have them all share a single certificate that lists each of the hosts as an alt. name.

When invoked, `certbot-auto` emits a deprication warning about python 2.6.  You can silence that by creating `/usr/lib64/python2.6/sitecustomize.py` with this:
```
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore", ".*A future version of cryptography will drop support for Python 2.6", DeprecationWarning, "cryptography")
# Use this instead if you don't want any deprecations:
# warnings.simplefilter("ignore", DeprecationWarning)
```
It is possible to run `certbot-auto` as a normal user, and it will try to use `sudo` as it needs to.  One problem, though, is that it needs to invoke `apachectl` which is located in `/usr/sbin`.  Unless you take steps to assure that `/usr/sbin` is in the `PATH` variable inside the sudo environment, the invocation will fail, claiming that the apache plugin is not working.  An example of a possible solution would be to add some variation of the following (depending on your security requirements) to `/etc/sudoers` (using `visudo`):
```
%wheel  ALL=(ALL)       NOPASSWD: ALL
Defaults:%wheel    !env_reset
```
Then add the normal user to group 'wheel'.  Then add `PATH="$PATH:/usr/sbin"` as an additional environment variable when invoking `certbot-auto`.

Thanks for reporting the issue.

Digging in to the source code, it seems that different implementations of the `retriever` method make different assumptions about the meaning of the second argument. `google.datalab.bigquery.Table` is the only one that assumes it is a running count of items. `google.datalab.bigquery.Datasets`, `google.datalab.ml.Models`, `google.datalab.ml.ModelVersions`, and `google.datalab.ml.Jobs` all assume it is a page size (which is the current behavior). The other clients in the `pydatalab` library all ignore the second argument.

As it is, simply changing the second argument to implement a running count would not be sufficient. We need to first make all of the retriever methods consistent with the meaning of the second argument.

I'll put together a PR to update the retriever methods.

> If your concern about API calls resulting in an event being appended to queueids_to_queues before the existing items which are already there, i think this is not the case.

Yes, that is the race I'm concerned with. When a local action adds a new message to the **transport's queue** prior to it being properly initialized.

> Since both transports (matrix & udp) get a ref to queueids_to_queues which means updates to the items will be seen by all parties who share this reference.

The code in the link does not have a synchronization problem because we are following these steps synchronously:

- restore the latest snapshot state
- apply the pending events from the blockchain

At this point the off-chain state is synchronized with the on-chain state, and the queue will be correct state. Additionally we assume the cooperative schedule nature of the greenlets + the complete lack of IO inside the state machine code, which means that operations that change that queue will be atomically executed on top of the pristine state.

>  In matrix and udp queueids_to_queues is processed to dispatch existing items and most probably any items that get appended to the queues between "start" and the "send" greenlet.

The queue passed to the `start` must not be messed with. Ideally it would be a read-only reference to a given snapshot of the queue or a copy.

The protocols don't actually keep a reference to the queues, that queue is just used during start up to send messages which are pending from the last run, the new messages are then sent using [MatrixTransport.send_async](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/7684f387e01c6482616bff5ce3d12573685e9ae6/raiden/network/transport/matrix.py#L248-L253) or [UDPTransport.send_async](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/7684f387e01c6482616bff5ce3d12573685e9ae6/raiden/network/transport/udp/udp_transport.py#L342-L347), and since the `send_async` and `start` methods don't have synchronization, a race can happen there.

Looking at the content of the error, it looks like there's something up with the OpenSSL crypto library. Specifically, the Elliptical Curve (EC_KEY) functions may not be available. That can be a problem for some platforms like Android, which ships a stripped down OpenSSL implementation that doesn't have EC support. 

It also looks like there may be a problem with the ffi library. It may be that you need to hand build those libraries, which would not be fun.

The old internal behavior can co-exist with the task model well because it does not have anything external (no task file, no signature) so what you gain is only a way to run part of the step in parallel. If we implement task file and signature, things can get complicated for cases such as if the same task has been running externally, if the task is killed externally, basically we will have to check the status of tasks, which can again be inefficient.

Fixed by recent changes to internal handling of targets.

Note that the internal targets of sos was a mess because we had files (`str`), targets (`BaseTarget`, `file_target` etc) and `Undetermined` (for missing). New features were added and the logics became more and more complex. Recent patches consolidated the logics and use `sos_targets` everywhere, which should make the core more robust.
